n_seagrass: Halophila ovalis and Enhalus acoroides form the meadow floor of Indo-Pacific coastal wetlands, fixing carbon at rates rivalling terrestrial forests through dense rhizome mats that anchor sediment and oxygenate root zones. Globally, seagrass meadows have declined by 29% since 1879, with Southeast Asian beds losing an estimated 30,000 km² to turbidity and eutrophication driven by coastal agriculture.
n_shrimp: Penaeus monodon — the giant tiger prawn — navigates between mangrove nursery and open estuary across its life cycle, converting detritus and algae into protein that supports every predator above it in the column. Thailand and Vietnam have converted over 2.5 million hectares of mangrove to shrimp aquaculture ponds since 1980, hollowing out the very nursery habitat the wild population depends on.
n_heron: Mycteria leucocephala, the Painted Stork, feeds by tactile bill-sweeping through shallow water — a foraging style that requires clear, fish-dense wetlands it can locate by sight from kilometers away. Colony counts at Bharatpur and across peninsular Thailand have dropped roughly 30% in two decades as wetland fish biomass collapses under eutrophication; when prey density falls below threshold, pairs simply abandon nesting attempts for the season.
n_runoff: Nitrogen and phosphorus loading from paddy fields and aquaculture effluent arrives as dissolved inorganic nutrients, triggering phytoplankton and epiphyte blooms that smother seagrass leaves and crash dissolved oxygen to near-anoxic levels at night. The Mekong Delta alone exports an estimated 450,000 tonnes of reactive nitrogen to coastal waters annually — enough to sustain persistent hypoxic zones that silence the benthic community for weeks at a time.
n_invasive: Oreochromis mossambicus — the Mozambique tilapia — is an IUCN '100 worst' invader that tolerates brackish water from fresh to near-marine, breeds year-round, and mouth-broods its young through conditions that wipe out native fry. Escapees from aquaculture now dominate estuaries across Southeast Asia, where they strip benthic algae, prey on juvenile shrimp and crustaceans, and out-compete native species for nursery habitat — a single pair can found a population that crowds out the food web within a season. Removal, not poison, is the only lever: sustained culling drives the colony below the density it needs to rebound.
